To: Members of the Executive Board
From: The Associate Secretary
Subject: Republic of Congo - Staff Report for the 1996 Article IV Consultation and Request for Arrangements Under the Enhanced Structural Adjustment Facility

Attached for consideration by the Executive Directors is the staff report for the l996 Article IV consultation with the Republic of Congo and its request for arrangements under the enhanced structural adjustment facility. A draft decision appears on page 22.

This subject, together with the letter of intent from the Congolese authorities (EBS/96/7l, 5/10/96), and the policy framework paper for the Republic of Congo (EBD/96/71, 6/4/96) is tentatively scheduled for discussion on Friday, June 28, 1996.
Mr. Hadjimichael (ext. 38583) or Mr. Bessaha (ext. 38763) is available to answer technical or factual questions relating to this paper prior to the Board discussion.

Unless the Documents Preparation Section (ext. 36760) is otherwise notified, the document will be transmitted, in accordance with the procedures approved by the Executive Board and with the appropriate deletions, to the African Development Bank (AfDB), the European Commission (EC), and the WTO Secretariat, following its consideration by the Executive Board.

1. Recent economic performance

Against a backdrop of continued sociopolitical tensions and security concerns, the Congo's economic performance during the first half of 1994 under the program supported by a stand-by arrangement from the Fund fell short of expectations, owing to policy slippages, weak management capacity, and exogenous factors (disruption of railroad transportation as an instrument of civil protest). However, since mid-l994 the authorities have made substantial progress in strengthening macroeconomic performance and in initiating far-reaching structural reforms in the public enterprise, oil, and financial sectors, and in the legal framework. Economic performance in 1995 and the first quarter of 1996 was broadly satisfactory, with most of the indicative quarterly targets observed.

text: 2. Report on the discussions and medium-term objectives and policies

Notwithstanding the improved macroeconomic conditions, the discussions on a program that could be supported by the Fund under the ESAF have been protracted, reflecting the daunting internal and external imbalances faced by the Congo. These imbalances called for draconian reform efforts on all policy fronts, as well as the establishment of a satisfactory record of performance and the securing of adequate external resources to cover the exceptionally large financing gaps. The discussions with the authorities focused, first, on an assessment of the progress made over the past two years; and second, on the formulation of a sufficiently ambitious but realistic medium-term adjustment strategy to address adequately the remaining large imbalances and structural weaknesses facing the economy, as well as mobilize the necessary external financial assistance.

The authorities' medium-term program aims at accelerating output and employment growth, attaining sustainable fiscal and external positions by the end of the decade, and achieving external public debt sustainability over the long term. These objectives are to be met through restrictive fiscal, incomes, and credit policies; a further containment of the domestic cost structure; a scaling back of the public sector's involvement in the economy through the dismantling of state monopolies and the privatization of public enterprises; and stepped-up implementation of a broad range of other structural and institutional reforms. The prospective increase in oil production from 9.3 million tons to 13 million by 1998 will ease the adjustment process, but a durable improvement in economic conditions will require the development of the non-oil private sector, an inherently protracted process given existing constraints.

The main macroeconomic objectives for 1996-99 are to achieve annual real GDP growth of more than 6 percent, consistent with a recovery in real non-oil GDP growth to almost 5 percent a year (compared with a population growth rate of 3.2 percent); lower end-period inflation from 5.5 percent in 1995 to 2 percent during 1997-99; and reduce the external current account deficit, excluding official transfers, from 25 percent of GDP in 1995 to about ll percent by l999. Attainment of these objectives is predicated on continued large investment by the oil sector on the ongoing major oil exploration projects and on modest increases in the investment by the Government and the non-oil private sector, with total investment averaging about 30 percent of GDP a year, financed with improved domestic savings. Because of the Congo's exchange arrangements, a combination of fiscal tightening and supply enhancing structural policies are at the core of the authorities' adjustment efforts. Attainment of external viability is predicated on the granting to the Congo of concessional debt rescheduling terms, and in due course a stock-of-debt operation.

The balance of payments projections show sizable financing gaps. The gap will be exceptionally large in 1996, given the need to clear arrears (CFAF 1,096 billion, or l0l percent of GDP), before declining to an estimated CFA 278 billion (23 percent of GDP) in 1997 and CFAF 2ll billion (15 percent of GDP) by 1999. The authorities hope to cover these gaps through additional commitments of nonproject assistance; a restructuring of collateralized debt service obligations; a rescheduling on concessional terms of outstanding arrears and current debt service obligations due to Paris Club creditors and other bilateral and private creditors (including Russia and commercial banks). To this end, the authorities concluded an agreement in early March 1996 on the repurchase of collateralized financial obligations to an oil company and have requested from Paris Club creditors : rescheduling of outstanding arrears and service obligations falling due during the period June 1996-June 1999 on pre-cutoff date debt on Naples

terms (with 67 percent reduction on a net present value (NPv) basis); they have also requested a deferral during the period of the ESAF arrangement of the outstanding arrears on post-cutoff date debt. However, even if the debt relief requested by the Congo is granted, there would still be residual financing gaps during the subsequent three years; these gaps are expected to be covered mainly by new commitments of concessional nonproject assistance. The debt sustainability analysis suggests that while the NPV debt/exports ratio and the debt service ratio after debt relief would fall below the threshold levels of 200 percent and 20 percent, respectively, in less than five years, but debt service payments due would still absorb more than a third of government revenue by 20oo. The Congo's external debt outlook would remain difficult.
3. Points raised in the staff appraisal

The Congo's performance over the past two years bear testimony to the authorities' commitment to reform and sets the stage for addressing in a comprehensive fashion the deep-rooted constraints facing the Congolese economy. The program is ambitious but realistic. The staff recommends approval of the Congo's request for a three-year ESAF arrangement and believes that the authorities' efforts deserve the financial support of the other creditors and donors as well. While performance so far has been very encouraging, four risks remain:
The broad range of reforms that are envisaged could severely tax the authorities' management capacity.
● The eventual costs of the redundancy payments for retrenched public sector employees could turn out to be greater, and relations with labor unions more difficult, than currently expected.
The improvement in the fiscal position is predicated on a strong performance of non-oil revenue and sustained cuts in the civil service wage bill and employment.
The residual financing gaps after the assumed debt relief during 1997-99 are fairly sizable and their closing might prove difficult.
The Congo's decision to accept with effect from June l996 the obligations of Article VIII, Sections 2, 3, and 4 is welcomed.
I. Introduction

The l996 Article IV consultation discussions with the Congo, together with discussions on a three-year program that could be supported under the enhanced structural adjustment facility (ESAF), were held in Brazzaville during November 23-December 7, 1995 and February 27-March 12, 1996. 1/ On May 27, 1994, the Fund approved a l2-month stand-by arrangement for the Congo, in an amount equivalent to SDR 23.l6 million (40 percent of quota). The program veered off track shortly after its approval and the first review could not be completed; the Congo made only the initial purchase of SDR 12.5 million under the stand-by arrangement. However, since mid-1994 the Congo has implemented a broad range of corrective fiscal measures, accelerated structural reforms, and strengthened its economic management capacity under Fund staff-monitored programs. The Congo's economic performance in 1995 and early l996 was satisfactory.

In a letter to the Managing Director, dated May 6, 1996, the Minister of Economy and Finance of the Congo requests a three-year arrangement from the Fund under the ESAF, in an amount equivalent to SDR 69.48 million (120 percent of quota), in support of the Congo's adjustment program for the period April 1996-March 1999, and the first annual arrangement thereunder, in an amount equivalent to SDR 27.79 million (48 percent of quota) (EBS/96/71, 5/l0/96). The remaining loan amount would be disbursed in equal amounts under the second and third annual ESAF arrangements (Appendix I). The proposed access to and phasing of ESAF resources is justified by the strength of the program, the large external financing requirements, and the low outstanding use of Fund credit. If all disbursements under the ESAF are made on schedule, the Congo's outstanding use of Fund credit will rise from SDR 12.5 million (2l.6 percent of quota) at end-March 1996 to SDR 70.9 million (l22.3 percent of quota) by end-March l999 (Table 1, and Appendix II). The Congo's adjustment policies for l996-99 are described in detail in the policy framework paper (EBD/96/7l, 6/4/96); the memorandum of economic and financial policies attached to the letter of intent also outlines the Congo's broad medium-term objectives and policies and highlights the specific policies envisaged for 1996.

The Congo continues to avail itself of the transitional arrangements of Article XIV. The last Article IV consultation with the Congo was completed on May 27, 1994 (EBM/94/48). 1/ In concluding the last Article IV consultation, Directors welcomed the authorities' decision to adopt an adjustment program capitalizing on the opportunities afforded by the devaluation of the CFA franc. In this context, Directors underscored the importance of expenditure rationalization and fiscal retrenchment for re-establishing policy credibility and strengthening the support from the general public and the international community. Directors emphasized that durable growth depended on successful public enterprise reform and liberalization of the economy. They also noted the country's large external financing requirements, which were due in large part to the heavy debt-service obligations, and encouraged the authorities to pursue with vigor a prudent debt management policy and regularize the Congo's relations with creditors.
Box 1. Data Situation

The availability and timeliness of the Congo's economic and financial data are broadly satisfactory, as a result of efforts made recently by the authorities to improve the statistical system. Stil, data weaknesses remain with respect to: (i) the quality of the national accounts and prices. National accounts are produced with a three-year lag, owing to untimely statistical surveys. The price indices are obsolete, as they are based on a household survey that dates back to 1964 and their coverage is limited to the capital city of Brazzaville; (ii) the coverage and recording procedures for government expenditure; (ii) the coverage and reliability of domestic public debt data; (iv) the comprehensiveness of the data on the balance of payments transactions of oil companies, and their reconciliation with the national and fiscal accounts; and (v) the measurement of currency of circulation. In addition, the dissemination of statistical data to the public at large is somewhat irregular. The authorities are stepping up their efforts to correct these shortcomings and produce a regular flow of reliable data to ease the monitoring of economic and financial developments. At the same time, the Fund and the World Bank are providing technical support in the areas of public expenditure management and domestic debt data, respectively.

The Fund and the World Bank staffs have been cooperating closely on the Congo. The World Bank supported the Congo's adjustment efforts in l994 with a $\yen100$ million economic recovery credit (June 1994), and accorded the Congo IDA-only status in September 1994 (Appendix III). 2/ The World Bank also approved a $\yen123,456$ million privatization and capacity-building project in

II. Background and Recent Economic Performance

The sharp oil price decline in 1986 and the associated deterioration of the terms of trade ushered in a protracted and difficult period of adjustment in economic policies and gradual transition to a market economy; the Congo was under a socialist one-party system and a centrally planned economy during l964-90. However, the policy response during the period 1986-93 to the worsened external environment was inadequate, relying mainly on cuts in government investment spending with limited structural reforms. As a result, economic activity stagnated, unemployment rose sharply, public sector and external imbalances widened markedly, the external public debt and debt service burdens grew to unsustainable levels, and sizable domestic and external payments arrears were accumulated. The adjustment strategy was broadened in January 1994 with the 50 percent devaluation of the CFA franc. Economic adjustment has been accompanied since l990 by a political transition to a democratic system of government. Multiparty elections were held during March-August 1992, but electoral disputes triggered intense political and social strife and armed conflict, which lasted until January l994. The next presidential elections are scheduled to take place in July 1997.

Against a backdrop of continued sociopolitical tensions and security concerns, the Congo's economic performance during the first half of 1994 under the stand-by arrangement fell short of expectations, owing to policy slippages, weak management capacity, and exogenous factors (disruption of railroad transportation as an instrument of civil protest). However, since mid-1994 the authorities have made substantial progress in strengthening macroeconomic performance and in initiating far-reaching structural reforms in the public enterprise, oil, and financial sectors, and in the legal framework. Economic performance in l995 was broadly satisfactory, with most of the indicative quarterly targets observed (Tables 2 and 3). 1/

On the fiscal side, the adjustment measures implemented during l994-95 included the implementation of the regional customs union (UDEAC) reforms, implying cuts in import tariffs to 5-30 percent; the lifting of all quantitative import restrictions; the introduction of a turnover tax; and large cuts in civil service employment (of about l0,o00 or 12.6 percent) and civil service salaries and benefits (l2.5-l5.0 percent), resulting in a sharp reduction in nominal terms of the wage bill (from a peak of almost 24 percent of non-oil GDP in 1993 to l6.5 percent in l995). These policies contributed to a major turnaround in the primary budget balance, from a deficit of 5.2 percent of GDP in l993 to a surplus of 6.l percent in 1995 (Chart 1, and Tables 4 and 5). However, domestic government arrears continued to accumulate, rising to CFAF 439 billion (44 percent of GDP) by end-1995, of which CFAF l42 billion (l4 percent of GDP) represented civil service salary arrears.

Broad money expanded markedly during $1994$ , but remained unchanged during l995, falling short of program expectations amid concerns about the financial health of the banking system (Chart 2, and Tables 6 and 7). 1/ Throughout this period, net bank credit to the Government was kept under control, with central bank advances leveling off--albeit above the statutory limit--while credit to the economy expanded modestly. All state-owned financial institutions in the Congo, including the social security agencies, have been facing major financial difficulties as a result of weaknesses in management, the buildup of nonperforming loans to the Government and public enterprises, and inadequate compliance with the edicts of the regional supervisory agency. With support from the World Bank, a comprehensive financial sector reform program has been under way since l994, aiming at the privatization, liquidation, or restructuring of all institutions.

The authorities have also been implementing or initiating a broad range of other structural reforms during the past two years, with World Bank assistance. In the oil sector, which plays a predominant role in the economy (accounting in 1995 for 33 percent of GDP, 85 percent of export earnings, and 53 percent of government revenue), a Hydrocarbons Code was adopted in August l994, establishing a transparent framework for activity and investment, and a level playing field for all investors. As required by the new Code, production-sharing contracts with virtually all oil companies have since been signed. In the public enterprise sector, a far-reaching reform program was initiated in l995, entailing the privatization or liquidation of all enterprises. The sector--which comprised 104 enterprises and accounted for 20 percent of GDP and 25 percent of formal employment at end-1994--has traditionally been facing major financial difficulties, owing inter alia to overstaffing and high cost structures. These difficulties have, in turn, impinged adversely on the quality of public services and hindered the development of the private sector.

Overall, the pursuit of restrictive fiscal, incomes, and credit policies in support of the devaluation of the CFA franc, combined with adverse exogenous factors (security concerns and transportation bottlenecks), led to a sharp contraction of real domestic demand in 1994 and a 5.5 percent decline in real GDP, despite some strengthening of net exports (Charts 3 and 4). Real non-oil GDP declined further by 7.4 percent, but recovered modestly in l995 (2.2 percent), aided by the resolution of the

bottlenecks in the transportation system, contributing to an increase of 1 percent in total real GDP. With the reduction in wage costs and the low increase in import prices in foreign currency terms, domestic price pressures were quickly contained in the aftermath of the devaluation. The 12-month consumer price inflation rate rose to 29 percent by end-l994, but fell to 5.5 percent by end-l995 (Chart 5); it edged somewhat upward, to 7.5 percent, in the year to March l996 owing to an apparent disruption in domestic food supply that caused high food price increases.

Balance of payments developments during 1994-95 were strongly influenced by trends in the oil sector and by the impact of the devaluation and supporting policies. They were also affected by movements in world oil and wood prices that gave rise to a cumulative worsening in the Congo's terms of trade of 15.5 percent. Despite a sharp decline in the volume of imports by the non-oil sector in l994, the external current account deficit (excluding official transfers), virtually doubled as a ratio to GDP, to 47 percent, as a result of a steep one-off increase in investment in two major new oil fields (Chart 6, and Tables 8 and 9). l/ The current account deficit narrowed to an estimated 25 percent of GDP in l995, as payments abroad by the oil sector fell sharply. The overall balance of payments deficit was contained in $1994$ but widened markedly in l995, as official and private capital inflows declined; after a large increase in l994, inflows of external assistance in l995 were practically nil, primarily because of the delays in concluding the discussions on a Fund-supported program. The balance of payments deficit and the clearing of outstanding external payments arrears, to multilateral and official bilateral creditors in $1994$ were financed essentially by a non-concessional debt rescheduling by Paris Club creditors and by debt cancellation from France. However, the widening financing needs in l995 gave rise to a further accumulation of external payments arrears, to the equivalent of 92 percent of GDP at end-1995.
III． Report on the Discussions and Medium-Term Program

Notwithstanding the improved macroeconomic conditions, the discussions on a program that could be supported by the Fund under the ESAF have been protracted, reflecting the daunting internal and external imbalances faced by the Congo. These imbalances called for draconian reform efforts on all policy fronts, as well as the establishment of a satisfactory record of performance and the securing of adequate external resources to cover the exceptionally large financing gaps (Box 2). The closing of these gaps had been hindered until early 1996 by the onerous terms of the Congo's external debt that had been collateralized against oil royalties. 1/

The discussions with the authorities focused, first, on an assessment of the progress made over the past two years; and second, on the formulation of a sufficiently ambitious but realistic medium-term adjustment strategy to adequately address the remaining large imbalances and structural weaknesses facing the economy, as well as mobilize the necessary external financial assistance. The authorities, including those at the highest political level, underscored their strong commitment to adjustment, as evidenced by the progress made since mid-l994. They emphasized that attainment of satisfactory output and employment growth, the restoration of domestic financial discipline (including the clearing of government arrears and the resumption of regular wage and pension payments), and the reduction of poverty were priority objectives shared by all political parties.

The Congo's production and export base remains highly skewed, with export receipts and government revenue heavily dependent on oil output; the non-oil export base is also very narrow and consists primarily of wood and limited agricultural (sugar, cocoa, and coffee) and manufactured products. Fiscal and external imbalances remain large, while the external debt and debt-servicing burdens are unsustainably high. The compression of government expenditure and the poor composition and quality of the public investment program over the past decade have contributed to a dilapidation of the economic and social infrastructure and has aggravated the poverty problem; the Congo's social indicators, which were among the most developed in sub-Saharan Africa in the early l980s, have since deteriorated substantially. The exodus from rural areas has raised the urbanization rate to 66 percent of the population, while the unemployment rate has risen sharply. The public enterprise sector, which together with the Central Government used to employ the bulk of the labor force, has become grossly inefficient. The private sector in the Congo is still embryonic, accounting for only one sixth of total formal employment. The development of the non-oil private sector remains impeded by inadequate economic and financial infrastructure, lack of entrepreneurship and low human capital development, the existence of state monopolies, and other structural and institutional rigidities.
Box 2. Key Measures Implemented Since Early 1995

The following measures have been implemented by the authorities since early 1995 with a view to establishing a solid basis for embarking on a medium-term Fund-supported program. Most of these measures were prior actions for the completion of the ESAF discussions.

Large cuts in civil service salaries and benefits (12.5-15.0 percent) with effect from April 1995. Full implementation of the UDEAC indirect tax/customs tariff reforms by March 1995. Increase in the main turnover tax (TCA) rate to 15.25 percent in March 1995, and unification of the TCA rates at 17 percent in March 1996. Adoption of a system of taxpayer identification numbers in November 1995. Establishment of two special units for the collection of domestic taxes from large enterprises in November 1995 and effective commencement of their operations in April 1996. Promulgation of a government budget for 1996, consistent with the program's macroeconomic framework, in February 1996. Satisfactory fiscal performance during the first three months of 1996, consistent with the budget targets for the year as a whole. Enactment of revised Investment and Labor Codes in January 1996. Completion of the renegotiation of the special tax agreements with major companies by early May 1996. Elimination of the single tax regime enjoyed by two enterprises in May 1996. Appointment of legal liquidators (designated by the COBAC) for two insolvent commercial banks in January 1996. Full harmonization of the domestic legal and administrative arrangements with the regional legal framework for the supervision of banks (COBAC) and insurance companies (CIMA) in February 1996. Adoption of an emergency plan to facilitate the resumption of the regular payment of pension and other benefits by the social security institutions in March 1996. Appointment of liquidators for 53 inactive or nonviable public enterprises by mid-April 1996.   
? Conciusion of an agreement for the buy-back of collateralized  obligations to an oil company in March 1996.   
? Establishment of a ten-member ministerial committee for the coordination and the monitoring of implementation of structural adjustment policies, and of a technical support unit in November 1995.
1. Overall strategy

The authorities' medium-term program aims at accelerating output and employment growth, attaining viable fiscal and external positions by the end of the decade, and achieving external public debt sustainability over the long term. These objectives are to be met through appropriately restrictive fiscal, incomes, and credit policies; a further containment of the domestic cost structure; a scaling back of the public sector's involvement in the economy through the dismantling of state monopolies and the privatization of public enterprises; and stepped-up implementation of a broad range of other structural and institutional reforms. These policies would be instrumental in establishing an enabling environment for private sector development. The prospective increase in oil production from 9.3 million tons to 13 million by l998 will ease the adjustment process, but a durable improvement in economic conditions will require the development of the non-oil private sector, an inherently protracted process, given existing constraints.

The main macroeconomic objectives for $1996-99\ 11$ are to achieve annual real GDP growth of more than 6 percent, consistent with a recovery in real non-oil GDP growth to almost 5 percent a year (compared with a population growth rate of 3.2 percent); lower end-period consumer price inflation from 5.5 percent in 1995 to 2 percent during 1997-99; and reduction the external current account deficit, excluding official transfers, to about 1l percent by l999. Attainment of these objectives will require continued large investment by the oil sector on the ongoing major oil exploration activity and modest increases in the investment by the Government and the non-oil private sector, financed by improved domestic savings, with total investment averaging about 30 percent of GDP a year (Table i0). 2/ Because of the Congo's exchange arrangements, a combination of fiscal tightening and supply enhancing structural policies are to be at the core of the authorities' adjustment efforts. As is shown below, the attainment of external viability is predicated on the granting to the Congo of concessional debt rescheduling terms, and in due course a stock-of-debt operation.
2. Fiscal policy

The primary objective of fiscal policy is gradually to achieve external viability by lowering fiscal imbalances to levels that, after the end of the decade, can be covered without recourse to exceptional financing. To bring about the required reduction in the public debt/GDP ratio, the primary budget surplus is targeted to rise further, from 6 percent of GDP in 1995 to

16 percent by 1999. 1/ In its microeconomic dimension, fiscal policy will seek to reduce the distortions in economic incentives, promote economic growth, stimulate private sector development, and reduce poverty. These objectives are to be achieved through tax reforms, improvements in the composition of government expenditure, a gradual reduction in domestic arrears, and enhanced government financial management.

The improvement in the fiscal position is to encompass both an increase in revenue (excluding grants), from 25 percent of GDP in 1995 to 32 percent by 1999, and an additional reduction in noninterest expenditure, from almost 20 percent of GDP to l6 percent, respectively. The revenue gains are expected to stem from an improved performance of both oil and non-oil revenue. Oil revenue should be boosted by the projected expansion in oil production and the introduction of the new tax regime for oil companies provided for by the production-sharing contracts; in this framework, oil revenue (excluding the one-off bonuses for the granting of oil exploration permits) is expected to rise from 17 percent of oil exports in 1995 to at least 32 percent after 1996. Non-oil revenue is targeted to increase from 17.5 percent of non-oil GDP in 1995 to 23.5 percent by 1999, with half of the gains expected to be achieved in l996. The higher revenue is expected to result from a further broadening of the tax base, additional tax reforms (an increase in the turnover tax (TCA) rate in l996 and the introduction of a value-added tax (VAT) in l998), the projected recovery in economic activity, and a further strengthening of tax administration (for details, see Section IV).

On the expenditure side, policies will be directed at shifting the composition of total outlays away from wages and salaries and subsidies and transfers toward priority spending on education and health, operations and maintenance, and public investment. The envisaged cuts in noninterest spending are expected to result essentially from a further reduction in the civil service wage bill, from 16.5 percent of non-oil GDP to 10.4 percent by 1999, through cuts in employment and continued wage restraint. Despite the decline in the past two years, civil service employment remains at

unsustainably high levels, while its productivity is considered very low; in relation to the population, the Congo's civil service is among the largest in sub-Saharan Africa. Accordingly, under the civil service reform program, employment levels are to be reduced from 70,600 at end-l995 to some 61,000 by end-l999 through strict control over new recruitment, natural attrition, early retirements, the elimination of remaining anomalies, and layoffs. l/ The envisaged public enterprise reforms are expected to obviate the need for budgetary subsidies, while scholarship subsidies would be limited to students with satisfactory performance.

Within budgetary constraints, the authorities will strive to make adequate provisions for priority spending. With effect from the 1997 budget, allocations for nonwage spending on education and health will be raised in real terms and as a share of total spending, so as to address effectively the needs for improvements in human capital, reduction of poverty, and alleviation of the impact of adjustment on the vulnerable social groups. Core public investment will also be raised as a ratio to GDP, from 3 percent in l995 to 5 percent by 1999, and will focus on the rehabilitation and expansion of economic and social infrastructure. Public investment, and the associated maintenance outlays, could be adjusted upward in subsequent years, to the extent new commitments are secured for concessional external project assistance consistent with the authorities' development strategy.

The Government intends to verify by end-1996 the validity of outstanding government domestic arrears. All validated arrears are to be eliminated by end-1999, primarily through cash payments, except for civil service salary arrears; the modest wage arrears built up in l995 were eliminated in early l996, while the l994 arrears will be cleared only to the extent that budgetary resources permit. More generally, to minimize the risks of deviations from the fiscal targets, under the program the authorities will limit expenditure commitments to the resources available; as a contingency mechanism, commitments for nonwage and noninterest expenditure are to be cut to offset any shortfalls in government revenue or external nonproject assistance, while protecting social outlays.
3. Monetary policy and financial sector reform

Monetary policy by the regional central bank (BEAC) will continue to be directed at validating the parity of the CFA franc vis-a-vis the French franc. Given this nominal anchor, inflation should be expected to fall gradually to the rate prevailing in France. The ongoing efforts to restructure the banking system are expected to enhance private sector confidence, and thus boost the demand for money. On this basis, the program for 1996-99 assumes a stabilization of average velocity in 1996, with modest declines in subsequent years, giving rise to annual broad money increases of

7-12 percent. l/ Within a restrictive overall credit policy, net borrowing by the Government from the commercial banks is programmed to level off and central bank advances to decline; total net credit to the Government is targeted to increase only modestly, through the partial on-lending by the BEAC of IMF credits. This would facilitate an expansion of credit to the economy at a rate somewhat faster than the growth in nominal non-oil GDP, while allowing for some contribution by the Congo to reserve accumulation by the' BEAC.

The principal objectives of the monetary authorities in the period ahead are, first, to rehabilitate the banking system and restore confidence; and second, to continue to enhance the effectiveness of monetary policy through an increasing recourse to market-based policy instruments. As regards the first objective, the authorities have already strengthened the enforcement of regional bank supervision and prudential requirements. They will strive to ensure continued strict compliance with these requirements in the period ahead. The staff pointed out in this context that compliance was principally a question of domestic political will to accept and enforce the edicts of the regional supervisory agency (CoBAC). The authorities are keenly aware of the urgency of restructuring the banking system, where only two commercial banks are still functioning, both of which are facing liquidity problems. Discussions are at an advanced stage with foreign partners for the privatization of one bank (UCB) before end-September 1996; discussions on the privatization of the other bank (BIDC) collapsed in early 1996, as the foreign partner withdrew for reasons unrelated to the Congo, and the authorities are currently seeking other potential investors. With assistance from the World Bank, all other state-owned financial institutions are to be privatized or liquidated by end-September l997; the objective is to reduce state equity participation to 20 percent at most.

The second objective is being addressed by the ongoing monetary policy reforms implemented by the regional central bank. The pace of these reforms, however, has been somewhat slow. Accordingly, the authorities have agreed to push within the BEAC for an early liberalization of interest rates and the discontinuation of central bank refinancing of medium-term bank credits (through "Window B") and of the access of nonbank financial institutions to central bank refinancing. These central bank operations, which are more akin to those of a development bank, have been justified by the BEAC on the basis of the limited development so far of the regional financial and money markets. The staff stressed the importance of increased reliance on market-based instruments for stimulating the development of the money market. As an interim measure, the authorities and the BEAC agreed to essentially limit Window B refinancing at the outstanding modest level.
Given the liquidity constraints faced by commercial banks in the Congo, very limited sales of BEAC certificates have been made since the introduction of reverse auctions in January 1996.
4. Other. structural reforms

To unlock the Congo's growth potential in the non-oil sector and stimulate the development of the private sector, the program will entail, besides reforms in the civil service and the financial sector, a frontloaded range of reforms in the public enterprise sector and a further liberalization of the legal and institutional framework. l/ The civil service reforms are expected to enhance the effectiveness of the central administration and, together with the emphasis on decentralization, should strengthen the provision of government services at the regional level and help reverse the migration flows to urban areas. The financial sector reforms should be instrumental in promoting domestic savings mobilization, improving access to bank credit, and deepening the financial intermediatior process.

The public enterprise reforms are aimed at dismantling monopolies and divesting the State from directly productive activities, and thus opening up opportunities for private sector expansion; improving the provision and lowering the cost of key services (e.g., electricity, water, telecommunications, and transportation); and reducing the potential financial drain on the budget. The reform program focuses on the outright privatization, or the privatization of the management and operations, of the six largest (pilot) public enterprises.

The civil service and public enterprise reforms are likely to result ir labor retrenchment. The potential costs of severance benefits for the retrenched employees of a number of public enterprises could be substantial under the existing collective agreements, which are considered unduly generous. To help contain these costs, as well as maintain the domestic political consensus for reform, labor union representatives have been invited by the authorities to participate in the deliberations of the privatization steering committee and in a special consultative commission, chaired by the Prime Minister and comprising trade union leaders and key cabinet ministers. However, despite an intensive public relations campaign by the authorities, relations between the Government and certain trade unions remain tense, reflecting the high degree of politicization of trade unions under previous regimes. In view of the current budget constraints, the authorities intend to initiate discussions with labor unions at the individual enterprise level once the restructuring plans have been finalized, so as to limit retrenchment costs to each public enterprise's ability to pay.

Overall, the fiscal program includes provisions for redundancy payments equivalent to CFAF 52 billion during 1996-97 (about $3.4$ percent of GDP a year), and CFAF l5 billion a year in the subsequent three years. l/ These costs would be covered in part by privatization proceeds, estimated at CFAF 15 billion in l996; any additional privatization proceeds for l997 and beyond, which cannot not be quantified at this stage, would be used either to supplement or accelerate the payment of redundancy benefits or to help cover the financing gap or speed up the elimination of domestic arrears.
5. External sector policies

The principal objective of the Congo's external policies is to further strengthen competitiveness and thus help restore external viability. The pursuit of restrictive incomes and credit policies, the liberalization of key sectors of the economy, and the expected reduction in the cost of infrastructural services should help maximize the gains in competitiveness resulting from the devaluation of the CFA franc. Such gains will be crucial for diversifying the production and export base. In the aftermath of the devaluation, the consumer price-based real effective exchange rate (REER) had initially declined by 38 percent, but by March l996 these gains had been reduced to only 10 percent, owing in part to the firming of the CFA franc against the U.S. dollar (Chart 7); however, changes in the REER tend to reflect to a large extent movements in the prices of internationally traded goods and are scarcely indicative of the underlying changes in domestic unit labor and nonlabor costs. Since early l994, nominal wage rates have been either reduced or frozen in broad sectors of the economy and considerable labor shedding has taken place; however, the cost of transportation and other infrastructural services have remained high. Given the structure of the Congolese economy, the supply response of the economy to the new economic environment is likely to be slow.

The trade system is free of quantitative import restrictions. With the effective implementation of the UDEAC reforms, import tariffs have been reduced to 5-30 percent; however, import surcharges of 30 percent apply on a range of goods that had previously been subject to quotas, thus raising the average import tariff. These surcharges are to be eliminated gradually over a three-year or a six-year period. Export taxes of 2-l3 percent apply to a small range of goods (wood and agricultural products), but the export taxes on agricultural products are to be eliminated by 1998.

External policies will also be directed at lowering the external debt burden to sustainable levels over the longer term through appropriate adjustment policies and prudent debt management, and at regularizing relations with creditors. Accordingly, the authorities are determined to refrain from resorting to any new non-concessional foreign borrowing,

particularly any new loans collateralized against government oil revenue (except for a loan currently under negotiation for the buy-back on substantially more favorable terms to the Congo of an existing collateralized obligation to an oil company). In addition, the Congo is aiming to clear the large outstanding external payments arrears by end-September 1996; no new external payments arrears are to be accumulated during the program period.

The Congo maintains a liberal exchange system for current international transactions and communicated to the Fund in early June l996 its decision to accept the obligations of Article VIII, Sections 2, 3, and 4.
6. Balance of payments outlook, debt sustainability analysis, and capacity to repay the_Fund

With the firm implementation of the policies described above, the external position is projected to improve substantially over the medium and long term. The volume of oil exports is expected to rise markedly, given the coming on stream of new oil fields and the associated 40 percent expansion in oil production between 1995 and 1999; on the basis of the current WEO projections, the growth in oil export earnings, however, should be moderated by the projected decline in world oil prices from ${\tt U S S17.2}$ per barrel in 1995 to ${\tt U S916.0}$ per barrel in l997, before recovering marginally thereafter. l/ With the easing of transportation constraints associated with the privatization of ATc and other supply-enhancing policies, the volume of non-oil exports is projected to increase at an annual average rate of 9 percent during 1996-99; wood exports are projected to recover by almost 70 percent between 1995 and 1999, albeit to a level that would remain still somewhat short of that in 1990. The volume of imports by the non-oil sector is expected to increase at a rate slightly faster than the growth in non-oil real GDP. On this basis, the external current account deficit, excluding official transfers, is projected to decline steadily, as targeted, from 25 percent of GDP in 1995 to some ll percent by 1999.

Notwithstanding the prospective large inflows of private capital to finance investment and imports, the sizable amortization payments due on public debt will result in capital account deficits after l996. Based on existing commitments from bilateral and multilateral donors, inflows of external concessional assistance would rise to CFAF 73 billion in 1996--comprising mainly CFAF 5O billion in program assistance from France, CFAF l0.7 billion from the World Bank, and CFAF 4.9 billion from the European Union--but would be extremely modest in subsequent years. As a consequence, large overall balance of payments deficits would continue to be recorded. These deficits, combined with the intended clearance of external payments arrears and a small buildup of gross official reserves, will exceed the net disbursements from the Fund and result in sizable financing gaps. The gap will be exceptionally large in l996, given the need to clear arrears--amounting to CFAF 1,096 billion, or l0l percent of GDP--before declining to an estimated CFA 278 billion (23 percent of GDP) in 1997 and CFAF 2ll billion (l5 percent of GDP) by 1999.

The authorities hope to cover these gaps through additional commitments of nonproject assistance; a restructuring of collateralized debt service obligations; a rescheduling on concessional terms of outstanding arrears and current debt service obligations due to Paris Club creditors and other bilateral and private creditors (including Russia and commercial banks). To this end, the authorities concluded an agreement in early March 1996 on the repurchase of collateralized financial obligations to an oil company (this repurchase would imply a reduction of some 50 percent in the net present value of these obligations); discussions are at an advanced stage on the terms of the new collateralized loan to affect this repurchase. l/ Agreements have also been reached with other holders of collateralized debt providing debt relief of CFAF 5.6 billion in 1996, while discussions are ongoing on debt relief for subsequent years. In addition, the authorities have requested from Paris Club creditors a rescheduling of outstanding arrears and service obligations falling due during the period June l996-June 1999 on pre-cutoff-date debt on Naples terms (with 67 percent reduction on a net present value (NPv) basis); they have also requested a deferral during the period of the ESAF arrangement of the outstanding arrears on post-cutoff date debt. The Paris Club has given informal indications that it would consider favorably a concessional debt rescheduling for the Congo. 2/ On the basis of the indications received by the authorities from several of their creditors and donors, and given the contingency provisions included under the program with respect to external assistance (including debt relief), there are reasonable assurances that the program for l996 would be fully financed. However, even if the debt relief requested by the Congo is granted, there would still be residual financing gaps during the subsequent three years, equivalent to 10.1 percent of GDP in 1997, 8.5 percent of GDP in 1998, and 6.5 percent of GDP in 1999 (see Table 3 of Appendix IV); these gaps are expected to be covered mainly by new commitments of concessional nonproject assistance. l/

On the basis of reasonable assumptions about the stance of financial policies and the volume growth of oil and non-oil exports, the current account balance is projected to strengthen further over the long term, and the Congo's external debt position to improve considerably. The debt sustainability analysis presented in Appendix IV suggests that while the NPV debt/exports ratio and the debt service ratio after debt relief would fall below the threshold levels of 200 percent and 20 percent, respectively, in less than five years, debt service payments due would still absorb more than a third of government revenue by 2000; 2/ this ratio would fall below 20 percent only by 2009. These results depend critically on the assumptions used with regard to debt rescheduling terms and the volume growth of oil and non-oil exports, as illustrated by the alternative scenarios presented in Appendix IV. In view of the uncertainties about the external position over the longer term and the large share of government revenue that would still be preempted by debt service obligations by 2000 even under the baseline scenario, the Congo's external debt outlook would remain difficult.

The Congo's outstanding use of Fund credit will rise modestly in the period to l999 in relation to total external public debt and GDP, to 1.7 percent and 3.9 percent, respectively, and should decline thereafter (Table 1l). Repayment obligations and charges should remain below 0.6 percent of export earnings and 1.3 percent of government revenue throughout the period to 2002. On the basis of the projected improvement in the external position and the fiscal accounts, and in view of the Congo's past good

1/ As the alternative scenarios presented in Appendix IV indicate, in the event of a nonconcessional rescheduling the residual financing gaps would be substantially higher than under the baseline scenario, by the equivalent of 3-8 percentage points of GDP a year during the program period and would remain high in subsequent years.

2/ Ratios of debt or debt service to export earnings are not very meaningful in the case of the Congo. First, export earnings reflect primarily oil export proceeds that do not pass through the domestic banking system. Second, as a member of the CFA franc zone, the Congo can have access to the operations account (gross reserve assets) that the regional central bank has with the French Treasury only to the extent that it has the domestic currency counterpart. As elsewhere in the CFA franc zone, the ratio of debt service payments to government revenue could be considered as an important supplementary indicator of the Government's capacity to pay and of debt sustainability. In should be noted that the ratio of government revenue to GDP in the Congo is fairly high (25 percent in 1995).
record in meeting its financial obligations to the Fund, the Congo should be expected to have no difficulty in discharging its obligations to the Fund in a timely manner.
IV. Program for 1996-97
1. Objectives and policies

Consistent with the medium-term framework, the macroeconomic objectives of the program for the first annual ESAF arrangement, which covers the period April 1996-March 1997, are to accelerate real GDP growth to 6 percent in l996 and 9.5 percent in 1997, on account of an expansion in oil and non-oil GDP; to lower end-period inflation to 3 percent in 1996 and 2 percent in l997; and to reduce the external current account deficit to 22 percent of GDP in 1996 and 16 percent in 1997. The growth of non-oil real GDP is expected to accelerate from 2.2 percent in l995 to 4 percent in 1996 and 5 percent in 1997. This pickup would reflect an expansion of activity in the non-oil export sector, as well as of activity in the nontradeable goods sector, as the resumption of regular payments of civil service salaries and of pensions benefits would stimulate domestic demand and permit an increase in capacity utilization. The main policy priorities are to strengthen the Government's management capacity, effectively implement the envisaged reforms in the financial and public enterprise sectors, and normalize relations with external creditors.

The government budget for l996 calls for a virtual doubling of the primary surplus to almost l2 percent of GDP, reflecting the prospective strong increases in oil and non-oil revenue and a reduction in noninterest expenditure in relation to GDP. The main revenue measures, which are expected to contribute two thirds of the increase in non-oil receipts, include an increase in the TCA rate to a unified rate of 17 percent; the renegotiation by early May of all special tax agreements (conventions d'établissements); the commencement of operations from mid-April of the two special units responsible for collecting domestic taxes from large enterprises; and the implementation from January 1996 of an all-tax-inclusive system of government expenditure. With technical assistance from the Fund, the authorities have also begun the preparation of a draft law for the replacement of the TCA with a VAT; the law is expected to be enacted by mid-1997.

On the expenditure side, the civil service wage bill is to decline from CFAF 11l billion in l995 to CFAF 100 billion (or from l6.5 percent of non-oil GDP to l3.8 percent), as a result of the full-year effects of the measures taken in early l995, a 4.5 percent cut in employment, and a nominal wage freeze. In addition, a Fund technical assistance mission in the area of budgetary procedures and government financial management visited Brazzaville in April/May 1996 and its recommendations are to be incorporated in the program at the time of the midterm review of the first annual ESAF

arrangement. 1/ Fiscal developments during the first quarter of 1996 were consistent with the budget estimates for the year as a whole (see paragraph 36 of the letter of intent for more details). The budget for 1997, which is to be discussed with the Fund staff in the context of the midterm review under the first annual ESAF arrangement by end-l996, will provide for a further increase in the primary surplus, to 15 percent of GDP, through additional revenue gains and a further cut in the wage bill to CFAF 95 billion (l2.1 percent of non-oil GDP).

The monetary program assumes broadly stable velocity and thus a pickup in broad money growth to 7.2 percent in l996 and almost 12 percent in 1997. Net credit to the Government is targeted to increase very modestly, on account of the disbursements from the Fund, but to allow for a reduction in gross central bank advances to below the statutory limit by end-1996. Credit to the economy would continue to grow somewhat ahead of nominal nonoil GDP growth. Monetary developments during the first quarter of 1996 were consistent with these projections.

As regards public enterprise reforms, the tenders for the privatization of the telecommunications company (ONPT), the petroleum distribution company (HYDROCoNGO), and the oil refinery (CORAF) were launched in mid-May 1996 ahead of schedule, and the privatization agreements with interested investors are expected to be completed by late l996; the tenders for the water (SNDE) and electricity (SNE) companies are scheduled to be launched by end-September l996 at the latest, with the privatization process expected to be completed by mid-l997. Given the complexity of restructuring the sixth pilot enterprise--the transportation company (ATC)--and breaking it into four separate entities, its privatization is likely to take more time and would not be completed before end-l997. At the same time, some 4l nonstrategic public enterprises have been identified for privatization, including about 20 by end-1996 and another 10 by mid-1997.
2. Program monitoring

To monitor progress in policy implementation, the program includes a number of quantitative and structural performance criteria and benchmarks, as shown in Table 12. The quantitative targets are subject to an asymmetric adjustment for larger-than-programmed debt relief and nonproject external assistance, as well as a contingency mechanism for deviations of government oil revenue from the program targets. The launching of the tenders for the privatization of five key public enterprises constitutes a structural performance criterion for end-September 1996.

After a disappointing start in the first half of l994, the Congo's economic performance has improved substantially under Fund staff-monitored programs. Since mid-l994, the authorities have tightened fiscal, incomes, and credit policies in support of the devaluation of the CFA franc; moreover, they have also sought to increase the ability of the economy to respond to the new set of relative prices by initiating or implementing a broad range of structural reforms in the oil, financial, and public enterprise sectors and the legal framework. The Government's management capacity has been strengthened, the primary budget balance has improved by more than ll percentage points of GDP, and civil service salaries and employment have been brought to more manageable levels. As a result, inflation has been brought back to low levels, and real non-oil GDP recovered modestly in 1995 after a large decline in 1994.

The authorities have embarked on a strong medium-term adjustment program aimed at accelerating output and employment growth, alleviating poverty, attaining sustainable fiscal and external positions by the end of the decade, and achieving external public debt sustainability over the long term. To attain these objectives, the program focuses on raising the primary budget surplus, so as to help reduce the unsustainably high external debt and debt service burdens; on increasing government outlays on education, health, and public investment, thanks to further savings on the civil service wage bill; and on stepped-up implementation of a front-loaded broad range of structural reforms that can restore confidence in the banking system, enhance financial intermediation, reduce the role of state monopolies, and improve the quality and reduce the cost of public services. These reforms should enhance the competitiveness and efficiency of the economy and support the growth of the non-oil private sector. In the view of the staff, the fiscal adjustment path and the envisaged structural reforms are ambitious but realistic. The satisfactory fiscal and monetary developments during the first quarter of l996 and the early launching in mid-May of the tenders for the privatization of three out of the five public enterprises targeted for end-September l996 bode well for the successful implementation of the program.

With appropriate policies and debt relief, the Congo's balance of payments position and external public debt outlook should improve significantly over the medium and long term. However, even after taking into account expected disbursements from bilateral and multilateral creditors, the Congo will face, given the intended clearance of the outstanding external payments arrears, an exceptionally large financing gap in 1996; sizable, although declining, financing gaps are also projected for subsequent years. The closing of these gaps and the attainment of external viability depend critically on the granting to the Congo of a rescheduling on Naples terms by Paris Club and other bilateral creditors of outstanding arrears and the service obligations on pre-cutoff-date debt falling due during the period to mid-l999, as well as a stock-of-debt operation in 1999 and the securing of additional commitments for nonproject financing. This reinforces the importance for the authorities to sustain a satisfactory record of performance. The results of the debt sustainability analysis suggest that even under these rescheduling terms, and despite the pursuit of strong adjustment efforts by the authorities, debt service payments due would still absorb more than a third of government revenue by 2000.

Sustained policy implementation will be imperative for achieving the medium-term program objectives and securing adequate external financial assistance. While performance so far has been very encouraging, four risks remain. First, the broad range of envisaged reforms could severely tax the authorities' management capacity. To minimize this risk, a broad range of prior actions has already been implemented by the authorities, but additional external technical assistance will be needed.

Second, the eventual costs of the redundancy payments for retrenched public sector employees could turn out to be greater and relations with labor unions more difficult than currently expected. To avoid the risk of excessive compensation claims and of social tensions, the authorities have to strengthen the dialogue with labor unions and the population at large; such a dialogue will also help broaden domestic support for reform.

Third, the improvement in the fiscal position is predicated on a strong performance of non-oil revenue and sustained cuts in civil service salaries and employment. To eschew deviations from the program targets, continued political commitment is crucial, and every effort should be made to broaden the tax base and enhance the effectiveness of tax administration and of government financial management.

Fourth, the residual financing gaps after the assumed debt relief during 1997-99 are fairly sizable and their closing might prove difficult. Attainment of debt sustainability will depend critically on the granting of concessional terms on debt rescheduling in support of sustained strong adjustment efforts by the authorities.

Overall, the staff believes that the Congo's performance over the past two years has been a testimony to the authorities' commitment to reform and sets the stage for addressing in a comprehensive fashion the deep-rooted constraints facing the Congolese economy. The measures already implemented by the authorities, combined with those planned for the period ahead, are substantial and adequate to achieve the objectives of the program for 1996-98. Therefore, the staff recommends approval of the Congo's request for a three-year ESAF arrangement and believes that the authorities' efforts deserve the financial support of the other creditors and donors as well.

The Congo continues to avail itself of the transitional arrangements of Article XIV, but its exchange system is free of restrictions on payments and transfers for current international transactions. The authorities' decision to accept the obligations of Article VIlI, Sections 2, 3, and $4$ is welcomed.

The Congo produces a broad range of statistical data for all key sectors of the economy, but improvements are needed in a number of areas. The authorities are strongly encouraged to address these weaknesses in the period ahead and to provide on a regular basis a range of economic data to the Fund, including the "core" data needed for surveillance.
It is recommended that the next Article IV consultation with the Congo be held on the standard 12-month cycle.
In view of the foregoing, the following draft decision is proposed for adoption by the Executive Board:
Request for a Three-Year and the First Annual ESAF Arrangement
1. The Government of the Congo has requested a three-year arrangement under the enhanced structural adjustment facility, and the first annual arrangement thereunder.

2. The Fund notes the policy framework paper set forth in EBD/96/71.   
3. The Fund approves the arrangement set forth in EBs/96/102.

text: Republic of Congo - Three-Year and First Annual Arrangements Under the Enhanced Structural Adjustment Facility

Attached hereto (EBS/96/71, 5/l0/96) is a letter dated May 6, 1996, with an annexed memorandum on the economic and financial policies of the Republic of Congo from the Minister of Economy and Finance, in charge of Planning and Prospects, and President of the Inter-ministerial Committee for the Monitoring of Structural Adjustment, requesting from the International Monetary Fund a three-year arrangement under the enhanced structural adjustment facility, and the first annual arrangement thereunder, and setting forth:

(i) the objectives and policies of the program to be supported by the three-year arrangement; and   
(ii) the objectives and policies of the program to be supported by the first annual arrangement.

To support these objectives and policies, the Fund grants the requested arrangements in accordance with the following provisions, and subject to the Instrument to establish the Enhanced Structural Adjustment Facility Trust:

1.(a) For a period of three years from [ .]，1996, the Congo will have the right to obtain loans from the Fund under the enhanced structural adjustment facility, in a total amount equivalent to SDR 69.48 million which will be provided from the Enhanced Structural Adjustment Facility Trust.

(b) The amount of the annual arrangements will be the equivalent of SDR 27,792,000 for the first annual arrangement; the equivalent of SDR 20,844,000 for the second annual arrangement; and the equivalent of SDR 20,844,000 for the third annual arrangement.
(c) Under the first annual arrangement:
the second loan, in an amount equivalent to SDR 13,896,000, will be available on -] 1996, at the request of the Government of the Congo subject to paragraph 2 below.
2.  The Congo will not request disbursement of the second loan specified in paragraph l(c)(ii) above,
(a) if the Managing Director finds that the data at the end of September 1996 indicate that:
the limit on net domestic assets of the central bank at the national level, or
the limit on net credit to the Government from the domestic banking system, or
the limit on the contracting or guaranteeing by the Government of new nonconcessional external loans with a maturity of more than one year, and a grant element of less than 35 percent, or
the limit on new nonconcessional external borrowing with a maturity of less than one year (excluding normal import-related credits), or
the target for the reduction in external payments arrears of the Government, or
the target for the reduction in government domestic payments arrears,
referred to in paragraphs 40 and 42 and specified in Table 2 of the annexed memorandum was not observed; or
(b) if the Managing Director finds that the Congo has not carried intention regarding:

the nonaccumulation of new external payments arrears described in paragraph 40 of the annexed memorandum, except on a temporary basis, obligations that are expected to be rescheduled, or the launching by [end-Sept., 1996] of tenders for the outright privatization or the privatization of the management of five pilot public enterprises (ONPT， SNDE， SNE，HYDROCONGO， and CORAF)，or
(c) if the Congo
has imposed or intensified restrictions on payments and transfers for current international transactions, or
has introduced or modified multiple currency practices, or
has concluded bilateral payments agreements which are inconsistent with Article VIII, or
has imposed or intensified import restrictions for balance of payments reasons; or
(d) until the Fund has determined that the midterm review of the Congo's program, referred to in paragraph 43 of the annexed memorandum has been completed.

If the Managing Director finds that any of the performance clauses that have been established in or under this paragraph 2 have not been met, the second loan specified in paragraph 1(c)(ii) above may be made available only after consultation has taken place between the Fund and the Congo, and understandings have been reached regarding the circumstances in which the Congo may request that second loan.

3. Before approving the second annual arrangement, the Fund will appraise the progress of the Congo in implementing the policies and reaching the objectives of the program supported by the first annual arrangement, taking into account primarily:

4.In accordance with paragraph 4 of the letter dated May 6, 1996, the Congo will provide the Fund with such information as the Fund requests in connection with the progress of the Congo in implementing the policies and reaching the objectives supported by these arrangements.

5. In accordance with paragraph 4 of the attached letter, during the period of the first annual arrangement, the Congo will consult with the Managing Director on the adoption of any measures that may be appropriate at the initiative of the Government or whenever the Managing Director requests such a consultation. Moreover, after the period of the first annual arrangement, and while the Congo has outstanding obligations to the Fund arising from loans under that arrangement, the Congo will consult with the Fund from time to time, at the initiative of the Government or whenever the Managing Director requests consultation on the Congo's economic and financial policies. These consultations may include correspondence and visits of officials of the Fund to the Congo or representatives of the Congo to the Fund.
