text: 3. Relations

The Congo's program with the World Bank was inactive between the time of its suspension in December of 1990 and the clearance of its arrears on June 29, l994, ending the nonaccrual status, which had been in effect since September of 199l. Following the devaluation of the CFA franc on January 12, 1994, the Bank's Executive Board approved an Economic Recovery Credit on June 28, 1994 in an amount equivalent to $\mathtt{U S S100}$ million, and accorded the Congo IDA-only status in September 1994.

The World Bank's proposed work program includes a structural adjustment credit during FY97 in addition to the credit approved on September 5, 1995 for privatization and capacity building. Project lending is expected in the health and transport sector.
1. Introduction

This appendix extends the analysis of the Congo's balance of payments outlook presented in the main text of the staff report to cover the period to 2015, with a view to facilitating an assessment of the sustainability of the country's external public debt position. The World Bank staff shares the assessment indicated below.

The Congo is one of the most heavily indebted low income countries in sub-Saharan Africa. At end-l995, the outstanding medium- and long-term external public debt is estimated at CFAF 2,794 billion ( $\cos5.6$ billion),or the equivalent of 278 percent of GDP and 418 percent of exports of goods and nonfactor services (Table 1). The net present value of the debt at end-1995 amounted to CFAF 2,898 billion $({\tt U S55.8}$ billion), or 289 percent of GDP and 433 percent of export earnings. The corresponding grant element was slightly negative, given the nonconcessionality of past borrowings; $\scriptstyle{\frac{1}{2}}/ $ in particular, that projected payments due over the next 15 years on an oil forward sale contract with an oil company, which constitutes the collateral for a l993 loan contracted by the Government, exceed the outstanding value of this loan shown in official debt statistics. Scheduled debt service due in 1995 amounted to CFAF 366 billion, or 55 percent of export earnings and 147 percent of government revenue (excluding grants). At the same time, external payments arrears are estimated to have risen by end-1995 to CFAF 919 billion (92 percent of GDP), including 23 percent to Paris Club creditors, 3l percent to other bilateral official creditors (22 percent to Russia), 34 percent to commercial banks, 6 percent to multilateral creditors, and 2 percent to other private creditors.

The structure of the Congo's outstanding external public debt at end1995 reflects the predominance of the debt owed to Paris Club creditors, which accounted for 53 percent of total debt; France is the Congo's principal bilateral official creditor, accounting for 63 percent of total Paris Club debt. Debt owed to other official bilateral creditors (including Russia) accounted for ll.5 percent of total debt, and was almost exclusively in arrears. The share of the multilateral debt amounted to 12.3 percent, of which 39 percent was owed to the AfDB/AfDF, 25 percent to IDA, 12 percent to the World Bank, 12 percent to the European Union, and 2.5 percent to the IMF. By end-1995, the Congo had accumulated external payments arrears to all multilateral creditors other than the World Bank and the Fund, equivalent to 16 percent of multilateral debt. Debt to commercial banks accounted for ll.l percent of total debt (all of which was in arrears at end-l995) and collateralized debt for another 9.7 percent, with the remaining 2.3 percent reflecting obligations to other private commercial creditors.
2. Main assumptions
The principal assumptions underlying the baseline scenario are as follows (Table 2):

Total real GDP is assumed to grow by 6 percent a year during the program period l996-99, 4.5 percent a year during 2000-2007, and 3.5 percent a year during the rest of the projection period, reflecting mainly the outlook for oil production; real non-oil GDP growth is assumed to stabilize at 5 percent a year after 1998.

· Gross domestic savings are assumed to rise from 26 percent of GDP in 1996 to 32 percent in l999, and remain near that level for the rest of the projection period. Gross fixed investment is assumed to increase from 29 percent of GDP in 1995 to 3l percent by l999 and to stabilize thereafter.

● On the basis of the expected coming on stream of new oil fields and the exploitation rate of existing fields, oil production is assumed to rise from 9.3 million tons in 1995 to 13.3 million tons by 1998, before easing gradually to 12.0 million tons by 200l; thereafter, it is assumed to increase steadily again to 15.4 million tons by 2007 and to stabilize at this level in subsequent years. Oil exports are assumed to follow the same trend.

● The volume growth of non-oil exports is assumed to average at 9 percent a year during the program period (l996-99), reflecting mainly a recovery of wood exports, and to stabilize at 5 percent a year during the rest of the projection period. As a result, the share of non-oil exports in total exports of goods would rise gradually from almost 15 percent in 1995 to 23 percent in 1999 and 34 percent in 2015. The terms of trade are assumed to improve marginally during the projection period.

· The elasticity of non-oil private imports with respect to real non-oil GDP is assumed to stabilize at 1.l over the next decade. With the expected improvement in the import substitution potential, the elasticity is assumed to decline to l from 2006 onward.

The Congo's contribution to the gross official reserves of the regional central bank is assumed to increase gradually, to the equivalent of three months of merchandise imports by 2002 and four months by 2011.

On the policy front, the Congo is assumed to pursue appropriate macroeconomic and structural policies, so as to maintain viable fiscal and balance of payments positions after the end of the current decade. As regards fiscal policy, the primary budget surplus is assumed to increase from 6 percent of GDP in l995 to l6 percent by 1999, so as to help reduce the debt and debt-servicing burdens; subsequently, it is assumed to decline to 10 percent of GDP by 200l and further to 4 percent by 20l5, as oil revenue declines (Table 3). Non-oil revenue is projected to rise from almost 12 percent of GDP in 1995 to 17 percent by 2002 and to stabilize at this level for the remaining period. Oil revenue would decline from a peak of 18 percent of GDP in 1998 to 15 percent by 2001 and then to 8 percent by 2015,owing to the assumed leveling off of oil production after 2007. Total government expenditure is assumed to decline steadily throughout the projection period, as a result of further cuts in the civil service wage bill and a lower interest payments burden; this would be consistent with higher spending on operations and maintenance and on public investment.
3. Results

Based on these assumptions, the external current account deficit, excluding official transfers, is projected to decline gradually, from 25 percent of GDP in 1995 to ll percent of GDP in 1999, 5 percent of GDP in 2001, and to 1 percent by 2006 (Table 4). It would then shift to small surpluses of less than $3$ to l percent of GDP for the remaining period. The deficit on the current transactions of the private oil and non-oil sectors is expected to continue to be covered by net medium-term foreign borrowing by the oil sector, inflows of direct investment in response to the envisaged structural reforms and the liberalization of the economy, and additional short-term capital inflows. The expected overall adjustment of the economy and the tight fiscal stance will help avoid an excessive rise in the debt burden of the private sector. Thus, the projected stock of external private debt would rise from an estimated level equivalent to 76 percent of GDP in 1995 to a peak of 97 percent of GDP in 2004, before declining gradually to 8l percent by 20l0 and further to 68 percent in 20l5; these trends would reflect in large part the prospective developments in the oil sector. On the basis of existing commitments, external concessional assistance would amount to CFAF 73 billion or 6.7 percent of GDP in l996, 15.5 billion or 1.3 percent in l997, and then would decline in the following years to less than l percent of GDP (reflecting modest project assistance). The overall balance of payments deficits, combined with the targeted clearing of the large outstanding stock of external payments arrears during l996, will give rise to fairly large financing gaps; these gaps would amount to CFAF l,096 billion or 10l percent of GDP in 1996 and, on a cumulative basis, to CFAF 1,83l billion during the subsequent three years.

Under the baseline scenario, it is assumed that the financing gaps would be covered during the ESAF period by a flow rescheduling by Paris Club and other bilateral creditors (including Russia and commercial banks) on Naples terms (with a 67 percent NPV reduction) of outstanding arrears and service obligations falling due during the period June 1996- June 1999 on pre-cutoff-date debt; outstanding arrears on post-cutoff-date debt to Paris Club and other bilateral creditors are assumed to be deferred during the period of the ESAF arrangement. In addition, a stock-of-debt operation at end-1999 with a 67 percent NPV reduction is also assumed on all remaining pre-cutoff-date debt that was not previously rescheduled or rescheduled on nonconcessional terms. The debt to commercial banks is assumed to be rescheduled on the same terms. On the basis of these assumptions, the 1996 program would be fully financed. However, there would still be residual financing gaps in the subsequent three years, equivalent to 10.l percent of GDP in 1997, 8.5 percent in l998, and 6.5 percent in 1999. These residual gaps are assumed to be covered by additional external nonproject concessional assistance (with an average interest rate of 2 percent and a maturity of 23 years, including 6 years of grace).

Overall, under the baseline scenario, the external debt position is projected to improve considerably. Key indicators of official external indebtedness are shown in Table 5. After debt relief on Naples terms from Paris Club and other bilateral creditors, debt service payments due would decline from 45 percent of exports of goods and nonfactor services in 1995 to below 20 percent (l8 percent) by 2000, and steadily thereafter, to below 10 percent after 20l0. The NPV debt-exports ratio would fall from 423 percent in l995 to below 200 percent (l6l percent) by 1998, below 150 percent by 200l, and would decline to 49 percent by the end of the projection period. However, the ratio of debt service payments due to government revenue (excluding grants), which is an important supplementary indicator of the Government's ability to pay in the case of CFA franc countries, would decline far more slowly than the debt-exports ratios. Specifically, the debt service payments due after debt relief would decline from 116 percent of government revenue in 1995 to 36 percent by 2000 and to below 20 percent only after 2007.

The balance of payments and external debt outlook is very sensitive to the assumptions used about the terms of debt rescheduling and the volume growth of oil and non-oil exports. Under Scenario 2, which assumes debt relief on Naples terms with only a 50 percent NPV reduction, the current account deficit would widen after 2000 relative to the baseline scenario by 0.5-1.5 percentage points of GDP a year. This would result in larger residual financing gaps during the program period (by l percent of GDP a year) than under the baseline scenario, and residual annual financing gaps of 1.5 percent of GDP in subsequent years. The NPV debt-export ratio would fall below the threshold of 200 percent in 2003, five years later than under the baseline scenario. Similarly, the debt service ratio will decline below 20 percent in 2002. In addition, debt service payments due after debt relief would still absorb 56 percent of government revenue at the end of the program period and more than a third throughout the projection period, significantly higher than under the baseline scenario.

Under Scenario 3, the Congo is assumed to be granted nonconcessional rescheduling terms (rescheduling over 15 years with 2 years of grace starting from end-l997, the same terms as in 1994 Paris Club rescheduling), without any stock-of-debt operation. The current account deficits would be significantly larger than under the baseline scenario after l996, as a result of higher interest payments, giving rise to fairly high residual financing gaps. In this context, the external public debt outlook would worsen substantially and become unsustainable; the NPV debt-export ratio would remain above 200 percent until 2004 and the debt service ratio above 20 percent until 20l2. Debt service payments due would continue to absorb 8l percent of government revenue at the end of the ESAF program and more
than 50 percent until 2007; this proportion would decline sharply in subsequent years, but will still absorb a third of government revenue at the end of the projection period.

Scenarios 4 and 5 assume, respectively, a 10 percent lower volume of oil exports a year and a 2 percentage point lower volume growth of non-oil exports a year than under the baseline scenario. Under these scenarios, the outlook for the balance of payments and the external public debt would also worsen substantially, with sizable residual financing gaps throughout the period to 20l5. The larger external imbalances and residual financing gaps would call for corrective policy measures; it should be noted that some adjustment is likely to come about from autonomous changes in private import demand and real GDP growth, as private disposable incomes decline. Nonetheless, while higher than under the baseline scenario, the NPV debt-export ratio would still fall below 200 percent by 1998 and the debt service ratio below 20 percent by 200o. The ratio of debt service payments due to government revenue would be slightly higher under Scenario 4 (because of the lower government oil revenue) than under the baseline scenario; the same should be the case under Scenario 5 if account is taken of the likely adverse impact of the lower growth of non-oil exports (and thus of GDP) on government revenue.
4 Conclusion

Overall, the Congo's external outlook depends critically on the assumptions made about the terms of debt rescheduling and the volume growth of oil and non-oil exports. Under the baseline scenario, the NPV debt-exports ratio and the debt service ratio after debt relief would fall below the benchmark levels of 200 percent and 20 percent, respectively, well within five years. However, debt service payments due would still preempt more than a third of government revenue by 200o, a fairly high proportion. In view of the uncertainties about the external position and the prospects for government revenue, the Congo's external public outlook would remain difficult.

text: Overall, the coverage of Congo's economic data is broadly comprehensive, but there are deficiencies in the compilation of national accounts, government finance, and balance of payments data. The authorities are taking steps to improve the quality and availability of economic data; in particular, they are receiving technical assistance from the World Bank, the European Union, and France to improve the overall national institutional capacity for the compilation, publication, and analysis of statistics. Moreover, the authorities are making appropriate arrangements to report to the Fund on a monthly basis on a broad range of economic and financial statistics in order to facilitate the monitoring of progress in policy implementation in the context of the ESAF-supported program. The coverage and the sectoral areas identified for improvement are described below.
1. National_accounts

Although no real sector data have been reported to the Statistics Department in recent years, the authorities do provide visiting Fund missions a breakdown of GDP on an output, income, and expenditure basis, both in nominal and real terms. These data are prepared by the Directorate General of Statistics of the Ministry of Economy and Finance. Actual data exist for the period up to 1992; provisional estimates of national accounts data are available for 1993, 1994, and 1995, prepared by the General Directorate of the Economy of the Ministry of Economy and Finance. Data on the oil sector transactions are subject to some weaknesses, reflecting the lack of transparency that surrounded oil operations until recently and the weak administrative capacity of the various institutions involved in the compilation of such data (the national agency of the BEAC, the Directorate General of Hydrocarbons of the Ministry of Hydrocarbons, and the Ministry of Finance). As part of the efforts to increase the efficiency of the oil sector, the role of the Directorate General of Hydrocarbons will be strengthened and its statistical capability reinforced.
2. Employment and unemployment

Detailed annual data on employment in the Central Government are available from the Ministry of Economy and Finance but they are not entirely consistent with the data from the civil service roster of the Ministry of the Civil Service. As part of the overall structural reforms undertaken by the authorities, the civil service roster is to be unified, with the assistance from the World Bank. Data on unemployment and the total labor force are not available.
3. Prices

A wholesale price index (wPI) and two consumer price indices, a highincome basket (CPI for European households) and a low-income one (CPI for African households) are estimated for the capital city of Brazzaville, and recently for the second major city of Pointe Noire. Data are compiled on a monthly basis. These consumer price indices are rather outdated, as they are compiled on a 1964 consumption basket survey. Both consumer price indices attach a very important weight to foodstuffs (57.9 percent for the African index and $50.4$ percent for the European one).
4. Government accounts

Detailed data are available on a monthly basis on government revenue, expenditure, and the overall fiscal position. These data, however, are subject to some deficiencies, as they are not always comprehensive in their coverage of expenditure on a commitment and cash basis. The latest data reported to the Statistics Department for publication in GFS Yearbook are for l980. No data are reported for publication in IFS. The coverage of data available to AFR is limited to Central Government, but also included until end-l995 some local government transactions funded by the central government budget (hospitals). In the context of the ongoing decentralization process, these operations will now be excluded from the central government operations. While some information is available on the activities of individual enterprises, no systematic data are collected on the financial performance of the public enterprise sector as a whole. As a result, it is presently very difficult to assess the impact of this sector's activities on the fiscal and monetary accounts. Some methodological problems remain in the presentation of the data. Preliminary efforts to address this issue include an ongoing audit of the Treasury with assistance from the French Treasury, and a review in April/May l996 of the budgetary procedures and government financial management by a technical assistance mission from the Fund; the recommendations of these missions are to be implemented over the next 12 months.
5. Monetary accounts

Monthly data on monetary statistics for the Congo, as well as for the other member countries of the Central African Monetary Union, are regularly reported to the Fund on microdiskettes by the regional central bank (BEAC). These diskettes are sent by regular mail, thus causing some delays; the latest was received in May l996 and deals with data for February 1996. However, more updated provisional data for the Congo are provided by the national agency of the BEAC in Brazzaville. Coverage is comprehensive and detailed, but the measurement of currency in circulation at the level of individual BEAC countries is subject to several deficiencies.
6. External and domestic public debt

The Caisse Congolaise d'Amortissement (CCA) monitors and manages comprehensive data on the outstanding stock of external public debt, including arrears, and its composition, together with detailed projections on debt service due. These data are provided to Fund missions. The CCA also monitors and manages domestic debt data. However, this statistical information is subject to some weaknesses. The World Bank is working with the CCA to correct these weaknesses; in this context, a full and detailed inventory of the outstanding amount of domestic debt, including all arrears, was completed at end-April 1996, and its validation is expected to be finalized by end-December 1996.
7. Balance of payments and trade statistics

The regional central bank BEAC provides annual data on exports and imports of goods and services and on capital flows other than public debt. The data are detailed and comprehensive through 1995. The Directorate General of Financial Control and Regulation of the Ministry of Economy and Finance has recently resumed the collection of data on external trade and is working closely with the central bank to improve the presentation of the balance of payments. In addition, quarterly data on exports and imports are compiled by a private foreign firm hired by the Government to improve the import valuation controls. However, the reliability and comprehensiveness of the data on private capital flows, particularly the short-term capital movements are limited, hindered particularly by the limited transparency of oil transactions and the weak administrative capacity. The sectoral breakdown of balance of payments data may not be consistent with the national accounts and fiscal data.
8. Public enterprise sector

There is no centralized comprehensive database on the operations of public enterprises. However, some information is made available to Fund staff upon request by individual public enterprises. Data are on an annual basis and record with a minimum six-months delay, reflecting the periodicity of the financial years of the enterprises.
